prediction.change: Inside the quote following "4/": the second glyph changed from ':' (dup) in Program A to '^' (over) in Program B — i.e. '[:*]' became '[^*]'.
In the return statement's third term, the operand changed from `c` (Program A: `p - q + c - d`) to `a` (Program B: `p - q + a - d`).
In the return statement's third term, `c` (Program A) changed to `a` (Program B): "+ c - d" became "+ a - d".
In the return statement, the third term changed from `c` (Program A: `p - q + c - d`) to `a` (Program B: `p - q + a - d`).
The comparison glyph after "3 8" changed from `<` (lt) in Program A to `=` (eq) in Program B.
The comparison glyph after "3 8" changed from '<' (Program A) to '=' (Program B).
The comparison glyph after "3 8" changed from '<' (lt) in Program A to '=' (eq) in Program B.

The comparison operator in the if condition changed: Program A uses `<` (`i * 2 < n`) and Program B uses `<=` (`i * 2 <= n`). A `=` glyph was inserted after the `<` on the condition line (line 9 in A / line 18 in B).
The comparison operator in the if condition changed: Program A uses `i * 2 < n` while Program B uses `i * 2 <= n` — an `=` glyph was inserted after the `<` (the operator became `<=`).
The comparison operator in the loop condition changed from "<" (Program A: `if i * 2 < n:`) to "<=" (Program B: `if i * 2 <= n:`) — an inserted "=" glyph right after the "<".
The glyph immediately after `[0][+]` (the recursion combinator following `4 2+6[0][+]`): Program A uses `&` (primrec), Program B uses `|` (linrec). A's `&` -> B's `|`.
The combinator glyph immediately after `[0][+]` (the 11th token / the character following the two quotes `[0]` `[+]`): Program A has `&` (primrec) where Program B has `|` (linrec).
The combinator glyph immediately following the `[0][+]` quotes changed from `&` (primrec) in Program A to `|` (linrec) in Program B.

In the return expression's first parenthesized factor, the operands of the subtraction are swapped: `(a - c)` in A vs `(c - a)` in B. Effectively the leading operand glyph `a` (right after the `(`) becomes `c` (with the trailing `c` becoming `a`). A gives (4-3)*20+2 = 22; B gives (3-4)*20+2 = -18.

In the return expression's first parenthesized term, the operands around the minus were swapped: Program A has `(a - c)` while Program B has `(c - a)`. Effectively the `a` and `c` glyphs on either side of the `-` are transposed.

In the first factor of the return expression, `(a - c)` in Program A becomes `(c - a)` in Program B — the operands `a` and `c` around the `-` are swapped (line 8 vs line 14). This changes the output from 22 to -18.
The 3rd glyph after the list literal `[6 2 9]`: Program A has `>` (uncons) giving `>_>__`, while Program B has `_` (drop) giving `>___`.
The '>' (uncons) at the 3rd glyph position after '[6 2 9]' in Program A is deleted in Program B (A: >_>__+ vs B: >___+).
The 3rd glyph after the list literal differs: A reads `>_>__+`, B reads `>___+`. A's second `>` (uncons), the 3rd symbol in that run immediately following `[6 2 9]`, is a `_` (drop) in B.
